text.title: Friction To Backlog Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Friction To Backlog application MUST enforce, plus a terminal skip-this-methodology rule.

rule.statement: Every backlog ticket emitted MUST cite a specific friction-id from the map. Tickets without a friction-id route to a separate 'unanchored' lane.
rule.rationale: Map-to-backlog traceability — anchors Friction To Backlog rule `ticket-cites-friction-id`.

rule.statement: Each ticket MUST estimate metric impact (KPI + ± delta + confidence). 'Improves UX' is rejected; '+5% checkout completion ±3% (medium confidence)' passes.
rule.rationale: Goal-Question-Metric — anchors Friction To Backlog rule `impact-quantified`.

rule.statement: Each ticket MUST cite ≥2 evidence sources (interviews / analytics / support tickets). Below the floor = the friction is conjecture, not observation.
rule.rationale: Evidence-required practice — anchors Friction To Backlog rule `evidence-count-floor`.

rule.statement: Tickets MUST be ranked by (impact × evidence-count) / effort. Manual re-ranks require a written rationale.
rule.rationale: Prioritisation-discipline research — anchors Friction To Backlog rule `priority-formula`.

rule.statement: The friction map MUST be re-synced weekly with the backlog: closed tickets mark friction nodes resolved; new tickets back-fill from new friction nodes.
rule.rationale: Sync-discipline practice — anchors Friction To Backlog rule `weekly-sync-with-map`.

rule.statement: If any Applies-If precondition fails OR any Skip-If condition triggers, do NOT apply this methodology. Emit a skip-record naming the failed precondition or triggered skip, then route the task to the correct methodology (or none).
rule.rationale: Mis-application produces worse artefacts than no methodology because downstream consumers trust the schema; the skip-record makes non-application traceable.
